question.text: When is a tax audit mandatory for a derivative trader under Section 44AB?

answer.text: When business turnover exceeds Rs. 1 crore (Rs. 10 crore if 95% digital) OR when the trader has opted for presumptive taxation under Section 44AD and declares income below the presumptive rate
Tax audit is always mandatory for derivative traders regardless of turnover
Only when losses exceed Rs. 50 lakh in a financial year
Tax audit applies only to equity derivatives, not to currency or interest rate derivatives

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Tax audit under Section 44AB is required when: (1) business turnover exceeds Rs. 1 crore (or Rs. 10 crore if 95% transactions are digital), OR (2) the trader opted for presumptive taxation but actual income is below the prescribed rate (6%/8%), OR (3) the trader has profits but opted out of presumptive scheme with turnover below the threshold. **The Trap:** Tax audit applies to ALL derivative trading businesses that meet the threshold — not limited to any specific derivative type. It's not always mandatory — small traders below the threshold with adequate profits don't need it. **Cross-Exam Context:** Tax audit requirements are covered in Series I Ch9 and VIII Ch9. They apply uniformly across all derivative segments. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** For derivative traders, the enhanced Rs. 10 crore limit (digital transactions) is particularly relevant since all exchange-traded derivatives are electronic — this significantly raises the audit threshold.
